_meta.authoringNotes: Pathogenesis (PLA2R, THSD7A, NELL1) per Beck NEJM 2009 (PMID 19571279) and Tomas NEJM 2014 (PMID 25394321). Treatment per KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Rovin Kidney Int 2021 PMID 34556300). Rituximab versus cyclosporine: MENTOR trial Fervenza NEJM 2019 (PMID 31293822). Ponticelli regimen historical evidence Ponticelli NEJM 1992 (PMID 1729518). Spontaneous remission rates Polanco JASN 2010 (PMID 20507945). KDIGO suggests anti-PLA2R titer monitoring; references Hofstra JASN 2011.

h1Title: Membranous Nephropathy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Membranous nephropathy (MN) is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults of European ancestry, accounting for roughly 20-30% of biopsy-proven adult nephrotic syndrome. It is driven by deposition of subepithelial immune complexes on the outer surface of the glomerular basement membrane, with thickening visible on light microscopy and characteristic spikes on silver staining. About 70-80% of cases are primary (autoimmune) and caused by circulating IgG4 autoantibodies — most commonly against the M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R, identified in 2009) in 70-80% of primary disease, with thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A (THSD7A) in 1-5% and NELL1, semaphorin 3B, and other novel antigens in the remainder. Secondary MN accounts for 20-30% and follows hepatitis B, autoimmune disease (lupus, Sjögren), malignancy (especially in patients over 65), and certain medications (NSAIDs, gold, captopril, mercury). Untreated patients follow the rule of thirds: one-third spontaneously remit, one-third remain with persistent proteinuria but stable function, and one-third progress to end-stage renal disease. Modern therapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide-based regimens, or calcineurin inhibitors achieves remission in 60-80% of high-risk patients and substantially reduces ESRD risk.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence approximately 1-2 per 100,000 adults in Western populations; accounts for 20-30% of adult nephrotic syndrome (KDIGO 2021)
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1; predominantly affects White and East Asian adults; rare in children
keyStats.avgAge: Peak age 50-60 years; bimodal distribution with smaller peak in 20-40-year-olds; older onset (over 65) raises suspicion of malignancy-associated MN
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global figure; estimated several hundred thousand prevalent cases worldwide given incidence and 5-year prevalence on therapy

definition: Membranous nephropathy (ICD-10: N04.2 nephrotic syndrome with diffuse membranous glomerulonephritis) is a histologic pattern of glomerular disease defined by subepithelial immune complex deposits on the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), with diffuse thickening of the GBM and characteristic spikes seen on Jones silver stain. The cellular mechanism involves IgG4-predominant autoantibodies binding to a podocyte antigen — most commonly the M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R), responsible for 70-80% of primary cases — at the podocyte slit diaphragm. Bound antibody activates the complement membrane attack complex (C5b-9) in situ, damaging podocytes and producing massive proteinuria. Newer antigens include thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A (THSD7A, 1-5%), neural epidermal growth factor-like 1 protein (NELL1, particularly in malignancy-associated MN), semaphorin 3B, protocadherin 7, and exostosin 1/2 (in lupus-associated cases). Primary MN is presumed autoimmune; secondary MN follows hepatitis B, autoimmune disease, malignancy, and drug exposure. Diagnosis is by renal biopsy with light microscopy, immunofluorescence (granular IgG and C3 along GBM), electron microscopy, and immunohistochemistry for PLA2R or THSD7A; serum anti-PLA2R antibody (ELISA or indirect immunofluorescence) has a sensitivity of 70-80% and specificity of approximately 99% for primary disease and is increasingly used to confirm diagnosis without biopsy in low-risk cases. Care is led by nephrology, with hepatology, oncology, or rheumatology input for secondary disease.

typesClassification.description: 70-80% of cases; circulating IgG4 autoantibodies against podocyte antigens (anti-PLA2R in 70-80%, anti-THSD7A in 1-5%, newer antigens in the remainder). Treated with KDIGO-recommended immunosuppression in high-risk patients.

20-30% of cases. Causes include hepatitis B (subepithelial HBV antigens), hepatitis C, syphilis, schistosomiasis, lupus (class V lupus nephritis), Sjögren, sarcoidosis, malignancy (especially solid tumors in patients over 65), and drugs (NSAIDs, gold, captopril, penicillamine, mercury). Treatment targets the underlying cause.

Up to 25% of MN in patients over 65 is associated with occult solid malignancy (lung, gastrointestinal, prostate, breast). NELL1-positive MN has particular association with cancer. Age-appropriate cancer screening is recommended at diagnosis.
WHO/ISN class V lupus nephritis with membranous pattern, often combined with proliferative lesions (mixed III+V or IV+V). Treatment follows lupus nephritis protocols rather than primary MN regimens.

typesClassification.type: Recurrent membranous nephropathy in renal allograft
typesClassification.description: Approximately 40% of patients with primary MN have biopsy-proven recurrence in a kidney transplant; anti-PLA2R-positive recipients are at higher risk. Rituximab is effective.

primarySymptoms: Foamy, frothy urine that persists from morning to evening — the visible expression of heavy proteinuria.
Generalized peripheral edema, often starting in the ankles and progressing to the thighs, scrotum or vulva, and abdomen; periorbital edema is common in the morning.
Sudden weight gain (5-15 kg) from fluid retention over weeks to months.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance from hypoalbuminemia and overall systemic effects of nephrotic syndrome.
Sometimes hypertension (40-50% of patients at presentation) with associated headache or blurred vision.
Microscopic hematuria in roughly 30-50% of patients; macroscopic hematuria is uncommon and suggests a different diagnosis.

Symptoms of venous thromboembolism — unilateral leg swelling with calf tenderness, pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, or flank pain — from renal-vein thrombosis or deep-vein thrombosis, particularly in severe nephrotic syndrome.
Dyspnea and reduced exercise capacity from pleural effusions or pulmonary embolism.
Abdominal discomfort from ascites or hepatic congestion in advanced fluid overload.

Symptoms of any underlying disease in secondary MN — joint pain and dry eyes in autoimmune disease, hepatitis symptoms in hepatitis B-associated MN, or cancer-specific symptoms in malignancy-associated MN.
earlyWarningSigns: Foamy urine that does not clear with a single flush
New persistent ankle or periorbital edema in an adult without obvious cause
Unexplained weight gain of more than 3 kg over 2 weeks
Mild dyspnea on exertion with leg swelling
Routine urinalysis showing 3+ or 4+ protein on dipstick
emergencySigns: Pleuritic chest pain or sudden dyspnea — suspect pulmonary embolism in a patient with nephrotic syndrome and proceed to CT pulmonary angiography
Acute unilateral flank pain with macroscopic hematuria — exclude renal vein thrombosis with Doppler ultrasound or CT venogram
Severe acute oliguria with rising creatinine — exclude superimposed acute kidney injury (intravascular volume depletion, drug nephrotoxicity, crescentic transformation)
Sepsis with peritoneal signs in patients with ascites — exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Severe hypertension (over 180/110 mmHg) with end-organ symptoms — emergency BP control

causes.description: Circulating IgG4 antibodies against the M-type phospholipase A2 receptor on podocytes bind in situ and form subepithelial immune complexes that activate complement and damage podocytes. Found in 70-80% of primary MN. Antibody titer correlates with disease activity and treatment response.

causes.cause: Anti-THSD7A and other novel antigens (primary MN)
causes.description: Thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A (1-5%), NELL1 (often malignancy-associated), semaphorin 3B (pediatric), protocadherin 7, and exostosin 1/2 (lupus-associated) account for most non-PLA2R primary cases. Discovery of new antigens has reduced the proportion of idiopathic disease.

Subepithelial deposition of HBV antigen-antibody complexes causes secondary MN, particularly in regions of high HBV prevalence (sub-Saharan Africa, Asia). Treatment of HBV with antivirals can induce remission of MN. HCV is a rarer cause.

causes.cause: Autoimmune disease (lupus, Sjögren, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease)
causes.description: Class V lupus nephritis is the prototype; Sjögren-associated MN, sarcoidosis-related MN, and IgG4-related disease are recognized. Exostosin 1/2-positive MN often signals lupus.

Up to 25% of MN in patients over 65 is paraneoplastic, most commonly with lung, colon, prostate, breast, or stomach cancer. NELL1-positive MN has the strongest cancer association. Age-appropriate screening (chest CT, colonoscopy, mammography, PSA) is mandatory at diagnosis.
NSAIDs, gold salts, penicillamine, captopril, mercury (including skin-lightening creams), and trastuzumab can induce MN. Withdrawal of the agent often leads to remission within months.

riskFactors.description: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1; men also tend to have higher anti-PLA2R titers and worse outcomes if untreated.
Peak incidence at 50-60 years; age over 65 increases the probability of paraneoplastic disease and worsens prognosis.
Strong genetic associations with primary anti-PLA2R MN (Stanescu NEJM 2011), with odds ratios up to 79 in homozygotes for risk alleles. Genetic testing is research-only at present.
HBV is the most important cause of secondary MN globally, particularly in children and young adults in endemic regions. Universal HBV vaccination reduces incidence.
Class V lupus nephritis and Sjögren-associated MN; control of the underlying disease often improves renal outcomes.
Particularly lung, gastrointestinal, breast, and prostate cancers in older adults. Treatment of the cancer often induces MN remission.
Chronic NSAID use, gold, penicillamine, captopril, mercury, and certain biologic agents have been implicated. Drug review is essential at diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 50-60 are the largest group, with male predominance approximately 2:1.
European and East Asian populations show higher prevalence; lower rates in sub-Saharan African populations where HBV-associated secondary MN dominates.
Children rarely have primary MN (under 5% of adult-onset numbers); when MN does occur in children, secondary causes (HBV, lupus, semaphorin 3B) are more common.
Patients over 65 are at higher risk of malignancy-associated MN; cancer screening is part of initial workup.
Approximately 40% of post-transplant patients with primary MN experience recurrence in the allograft.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with confirmation of nephrotic syndrome — proteinuria over 3.5 g/24 hours (or urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio over 3.5 g/g) with hypoalbuminemia under 30 g/L, edema, and hyperlipidemia. Serum and urine immunofixation rules out paraprotein-associated disease. Hepatitis B and C serology, HIV testing, autoimmune panel (ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement C3 and C4, anti-Ro, anti-La, anti-thyroid), serum anti-PLA2R antibody, age-appropriate cancer screening, and review of medications all proceed in parallel. Anti-PLA2R antibody by indirect immunofluorescence or ELISA is positive in 70-80% of primary MN and is approximately 99% specific. A positive anti-PLA2R titer combined with nephrotic syndrome and absence of secondary causes can sometimes confirm primary MN without biopsy (KDIGO 2021), but renal biopsy remains the gold standard. Biopsy shows diffuse subepithelial immune deposits on electron microscopy with characteristic GBM spikes, granular IgG (predominantly IgG4 in primary disease, IgG1/IgG3 in secondary or lupus-associated) and C3 on immunofluorescence, and positive PLA2R or THSD7A staining when applicable. Disease stages (Ehrenreich-Churg I to IV) reflect deposit location and basement membrane thickening. Risk stratification at diagnosis uses proteinuria magnitude, serum albumin, eGFR, anti-PLA2R titer, and rate of change — KDIGO 2021 separates low, moderate, high, and very-high risk to guide treatment. Imaging (renal ultrasound) excludes obstruction and assesses kidney size; chest CT, colonoscopy, mammography, and PSA are obtained based on age and risk profile.

diagnosticTests.test: 24-hour urine protein or urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio (uPCR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies proteinuria; over 3.5 g/24h or uPCR over 3.5 g/g defines nephrotic-range and orients treatment risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 24-hour urine collection at home or spot urine for uPCR; results in 1-2 days. Serial measurements track disease activity and treatment response.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum albumin, lipid profile, creatinine, eGFR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms nephrotic syndrome and assesses baseline kidney function; albumin under 25 g/L raises thrombosis risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Lipid profile shows high LDL and triglycerides driven by hepatic lipoprotein synthesis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses primary MN with 70-80% sensitivity and 99% specificity; serial titers track treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. ELISA result in days; immunofluorescence at reference labs. A negative test does not exclude MN — biopsy required to confirm.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal biopsy with light, immunofluorescence, electron microscopy, and PLA2R/THSD7A immunohistochemistry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard diagnostic test; distinguishes primary from secondary disease, stages disease, and assesses chronic injury
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anesthesia and ultrasound guidance, 30 minutes plus 6-12 hours observation. Major bleeding risk under 1%. Histology report within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Secondary cause workup (HBV/HCV serology, HIV, ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement, RPR, anti-Ro/La, IgG subclasses, NELL1 staining)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies treatable secondary causes that change therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood tests usually returned within 1-2 weeks. Positive findings (e.g., HBV) redirect treatment to antiviral therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes paraneoplastic MN, particularly in patients over 65; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, colonoscopy, mammography, PSA, and other organ-specific tests as appropriate
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Coordinated outpatient workup; specific tests guided by age, symptoms, and family history.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal vein and lower-limb Doppler ultrasound
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies renal vein thrombosis or DVT in symptomatic or high-risk patients (albumin under 25 g/L)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient ultrasound, 30 minutes; CT venogram or MR venogram if Doppler inconclusive.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is risk-stratified per KDIGO 2021. All patients receive supportive care: ACE inhibitors or ARBs to maximally tolerated doses to reduce proteinuria, sodium restriction and diuretics for edema, statin therapy for nephrotic hyperlipidemia, and prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk thrombosis cases (serum albumin under 25 g/L, history of thrombosis, immobilization). Low-risk patients (proteinuria under 3.5 g/day with normal eGFR, declining anti-PLA2R titer) are managed with supportive care alone, since one-third remit spontaneously within 12-24 months. Moderate-risk patients are offered immunosuppression after a 6-month trial of supportive care if proteinuria persists. High and very-high-risk patients (proteinuria over 8 g/day, declining eGFR, high anti-PLA2R titer, life-threatening complications) start immunosuppression at diagnosis. Three regimens have evidence and are first-line. Rituximab (1 g IV days 1 and 15, or 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks) has emerged as the preferred first-line therapy after the MENTOR trial (Fervenza NEJM 2019) showed superiority over cyclosporine for sustained remission at 24 months. The classic Ponticelli regimen alternates monthly cyclophosphamide and corticosteroids over 6 months and remains highly effective, particularly in patients with declining eGFR. Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine or tacrolimus) plus low-dose steroids induce remission in 60-70% but are associated with high relapse rates after withdrawal. Newer trials are evaluating obinutuzumab and felzartamab. Hepatitis B-associated MN responds to nucleos(t)ide analogues (entecavir, tenofovir); malignancy-associated MN responds to cancer treatment; lupus-associated MN is treated with lupus protocols (mycophenolate plus corticosteroids; rituximab in resistant cases). Long-term care includes annual proteinuria, eGFR, anti-PLA2R titer monitoring, cardiovascular risk management, and vaccination updates.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (1 g IV days 1 and 15, or 375 mg/m2 weekly × 4)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depleting B cells. KDIGO 2021 first-line for primary MN in moderate-to-high-risk patients. Often combined with anti-PLA2R titer monitoring to time re-treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission in 60-65% at 24 months (MENTOR trial, Fervenza 2019); 35% complete remission and 30% partial remission at 24 months

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide-based Ponticelli regimen (alternating months of methylprednisolone pulses + oral steroid and oral cyclophosphamide 2-2.5 mg/kg/day) for 6 months
medicalTreatments.description: Classic immunosuppressive regimen for severe or rapidly progressive disease. High remission rate; toxicity includes bone marrow suppression, infection, infertility, and bladder cancer risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission in 80-85% at 12-24 months in original trials (Ponticelli NEJM 1992)

medicalTreatments.name: Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine 3.5-5 mg/kg/day or tacrolimus 0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day) plus low-dose prednisolone
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line in patients unable to tolerate rituximab or cyclophosphamide. Nephrotoxic; relapse rate after withdrawal up to 50%. Useful in pregnancy and in transplant recipients with recurrent MN.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial remission in 60-70% at 12 months; high relapse rate after taper

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitor or ARB at maximally tolerated dose
medicalTreatments.description: First-line supportive therapy. Reduces proteinuria by 30-50% through reduced glomerular pressure. Often combined with sodium restriction (under 2 g/day).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Proteinuria reduction averaging 30-40% in months; up to 35% achieve under 3.5 g/day on optimized ACE inhibitor alone

medicalTreatments.name: Loop diuretic plus thiazide for refractory edema
medicalTreatments.description: Furosemide 40-160 mg/day with or without bumetanide and a thiazide; high doses required in hypoalbuminemic patients because of reduced drug delivery and albumin-bound diuretic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reliable diuresis in most patients with careful electrolyte and renal-function monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Statin therapy
medicalTreatments.description: All patients with nephrotic-range proteinuria receive statin therapy targeting LDL under 1.8 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) because of accelerated cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantial cardiovascular risk reduction; lipid normalization in 70-80%

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic anticoagulation for high-risk thrombosis
medicalTreatments.description: Warfarin or low-molecular-weight heparin in patients with serum albumin under 25 g/L, history of thrombosis, or other risk factors; DOACs increasingly used though evidence in MN is limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lee model and other algorithms guide individualized decision; thrombosis incidence in high-risk patients reduced significantly with anticoagulation

alternativeTreatments.name: Obinutuzumab and other anti-CD20 agents (clinical trial setting)
alternativeTreatments.description: Second-generation anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies under investigation for primary MN; early data suggest deeper B-cell depletion and possible advantage in rituximab-resistant disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) gel
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in some North American centers for refractory MN; small studies show modest remission rates. Cost and availability limit use.

whySeeSpecialist: Nephrology referral is essential for any adult with nephrotic-range proteinuria. Specialists confirm diagnosis with biopsy, identify secondary causes, perform risk stratification, and select immunosuppression. Tertiary glomerular disease centers offer access to rituximab and clinical trials of newer agents.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist with experience in glomerular diseases — KDIGO-aligned protocols, access to anti-PLA2R testing, and a working relationship with renal pathology, oncology, and hepatology. Tertiary centers with dedicated glomerular disease clinics or trial enrolment are preferred for high-risk or refractory disease.
hospitalCriteria: Day-case renal biopsy service with experienced ultrasound-guided operators
Renal pathology with electron microscopy and PLA2R/THSD7A immunohistochemistry
Anti-PLA2R antibody testing locally or via reliable referral
Rituximab infusion capacity and KDIGO-aligned glomerular disease pathway
Multidisciplinary input from hepatology, oncology, and rheumatology for secondary causes
keyFacilities: Renal transplant centers managing recurrent MN
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate against hepatitis B universally to prevent the most common cause of secondary MN globally.
Avoid chronic NSAID use without medical supervision; review medications regularly.
Maintain age-appropriate cancer screening, particularly after age 50.
Manage autoimmune diseases (lupus, Sjögren) according to specialist guidance to reduce renal complications.
Monitor blood pressure, lipids, and urinalysis in patients with risk factors or family history of glomerular disease.
Avoid mercury exposure (some skin-lightening creams, traditional remedies, occupational hazards) — a recognized cause of MN in some populations.
lifestyleModifications: Restrict dietary sodium to 2-3 g/day to control edema and blood pressure.
Take ACE inhibitor or ARB consistently at the prescribed dose; never stop suddenly without medical advice.
Adhere to immunosuppression schedule and infection prophylaxis (PJP, varicella, vaccinations).
Maintain regular outpatient blood and urine monitoring during active treatment.
Stop smoking; tobacco worsens cardiovascular and renal outcomes.
Engage in moderate exercise once edema and thrombosis risk are stable, with anticoagulation if indicated.

recommended: Sodium 2-3 g/day (less than 5 g salt) for edema control
Protein 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day in non-severe disease; higher with active losses, lower in CKD progression per dietitian advice
Mediterranean-style pattern with vegetables, fish, olive oil, and limited red meat
Adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg/day) and vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day during steroid therapy
avoid: Excess sodium (over 5 g salt/day), processed and restaurant foods high in salt
Excessive animal protein that accelerates hyperfiltration
Grapefruit juice in patients on cyclosporine or tacrolimus
NSAIDs and naproxen without medical supervision; they worsen edema and renal function

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic activity is safe and beneficial once severe edema is controlled. Patients on anticoagulation should avoid contact sports. Resistance training supports lean mass during steroid therapy. Restart progressively after rituximab infusions and check for any new infection or flare before increasing intensity.
dailyManagement: Weigh yourself at the same time daily and report sudden gain of more than 1.5 kg over 48 hours.
Take ACE inhibitor or ARB and diuretic as prescribed; do not skip doses.
Inspect legs daily for new swelling, redness, or calf tenderness (DVT signs).
Maintain immunosuppression schedule; avoid contact with known infectious cases during B-cell depletion.
Monitor blood pressure at home twice weekly during initial therapy.
Attend scheduled urine, blood, and anti-PLA2R titer measurements as agreed with the nephrology team.
prognosis: Outlook in MN follows the rule of thirds without treatment but is substantially better with risk-stratified KDIGO-aligned care. Low-risk patients have spontaneous complete or partial remission rates of 30-35% within 12-24 months. With immunosuppression, complete or partial remission is achieved in 60-80% of moderate-to-high-risk patients. Long-term renal survival at 10 years exceeds 80% in patients achieving sustained remission, versus 35-50% in those with persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria. The MENTOR trial showed sustained remission at 24 months in 60% on rituximab versus 20% on cyclosporine, with similar safety. Predictors of poor outcome include male sex, older age, severe baseline proteinuria over 8 g/day, declining eGFR, high anti-PLA2R titer, and chronic damage on biopsy (interstitial fibrosis over 25%). Recurrence after transplantation occurs in approximately 40% of primary MN recipients and is treated effectively with rituximab. Cardiovascular and thromboembolic complications remain significant contributors to morbidity and mortality and require integrated management.
recoveryTimeline: Proteinuria typically begins to fall within 6-12 weeks of starting ACE inhibitor or ARB, and within 3-6 months of starting immunosuppression. Complete remission (proteinuria under 0.3 g/day) usually takes 12-24 months on rituximab and 6-12 months on cyclophosphamide-based regimens. Anti-PLA2R titers fall before proteinuria, often within 3-6 months of effective therapy. Edema resolves within weeks of diuretic and protein response. Sustained remission after rituximab can last several years; re-treatment is timed by titer rebound and proteinuria recurrence.
complications: Venous thromboembolism (renal vein thrombosis, deep-vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism) in up to 25% of patients with severe nephrotic syndrome (albumin under 25 g/L).
Progression to chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease in 30-50% of untreated nephrotic-range patients over 10 years.
Accelerated cardiovascular disease from dyslipidemia, hypertension, and endothelial dysfunction.
Infections, especially encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, Hib) from urinary loss of IgG and complement factors, plus opportunistic infections during immunosuppression.
Adverse effects of long-term immunosuppression: bone marrow suppression, infertility, malignancy (cyclophosphamide), nephrotoxicity (calcineurin inhibitors), infusion reactions (rituximab).
Hypovolemic acute kidney injury from over-diuresis or sepsis superimposed on chronic disease.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guidelines 2021
supportResources.description: Comprehensive evidence-based guidance on management of membranous nephropathy and other glomerular diseases.

supportResources.name: NephCure Kidney International
supportResources.description: Patient organization focused on nephrotic syndrome, including primary MN, with educational and trial resources.

supportResources.name: American Society of Nephrology — Membranous Nephropathy
supportResources.description: Clinician and patient resources from the leading US nephrology society.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation Glomerular Disease pages
supportResources.description: Patient-facing summaries on diagnosis, treatment, and self-care for glomerular diseases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: FSGS shows segmental scarring of glomeruli with podocyte injury but no subepithelial deposits; immunofluorescence is typically negative for IgG. MN shows diffuse subepithelial immune complexes with granular IgG and C3 along the GBM. Both cause nephrotic syndrome; biopsy distinguishes them and treatment differs (rituximab/cyclophosphamide for MN versus steroids and CNIs for FSGS).

confusedWithConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Minimal change disease has normal light microscopy and only podocyte foot-process effacement on electron microscopy; classically a pediatric disease but seen in adults. MN is the commonest adult nephrotic-syndrome diagnosis with subepithelial deposits and characteristic GBM thickening; treatment regimens differ markedly.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA nephropathy classically presents with hematuria and episodic synpharyngitic flares; biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposits, not subepithelial GBM deposits. MN presents with nephrotic syndrome and granular IgG along GBM without mesangial IgA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic nephropathy shows mesangial expansion, glomerular basement membrane thickening, and nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson) without immune deposits; biopsy is required to distinguish from MN in diabetic patients with nephrotic syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Clinical syndrome that MN typically produces in adults; differential diagnosis includes minimal change, FSGS, and diabetic nephropathy.

relatedConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Key differential diagnosis for adult nephrotic syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Minimal Change Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Other primary cause of adult nephrotic syndrome with different histology and treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Class V lupus nephritis is the prototype secondary MN; treatment follows lupus-specific protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: Globally the most common cause of secondary MN; antiviral therapy can induce remission.

faqs.question: What is membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Membranous nephropathy is a kidney disease in which antibody complexes deposit on the outer surface of the glomerular basement membrane, thickening it and causing massive protein loss in the urine. It is the most common cause of adult nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic patients of European ancestry and is treated with ACE inhibitors and, in higher-risk patients, immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are foamy urine, generalized swelling (ankles, face, abdomen), unexplained weight gain, fatigue, and sometimes high blood pressure. Severe disease can cause renal vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, with leg swelling, chest pain, or sudden dyspnea — all requiring urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: How is membranous nephropathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a 24-hour urine showing over 3.5 g protein, hypoalbuminemia, and a renal biopsy showing subepithelial immune deposits with characteristic GBM spikes. Anti-PLA2R antibody testing identifies 70-80% of primary cases and is approximately 99% specific. Secondary causes (hepatitis B, lupus, malignancy, drugs) are excluded with targeted tests.

faqs.question: What causes membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: About 70-80% of cases are primary autoimmune disease driven by antibodies against the podocyte protein PLA2R (or THSD7A and other antigens). The remaining 20-30% are secondary to hepatitis B, lupus, malignancy (especially in older adults), and drugs such as NSAIDs, gold, captopril, penicillamine, and mercury.

faqs.question: Does membranous nephropathy go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Roughly one-third of untreated patients spontaneously achieve complete or partial remission within 12-24 months, especially those with proteinuria under 3.5 g/day. Another third stabilize with persistent proteinuria, and a final third progress to kidney failure. Risk-stratified treatment with rituximab or cyclophosphamide significantly improves outcomes for high-risk patients.

faqs.question: Is rituximab effective for membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rituximab is now KDIGO-recommended first-line therapy for moderate-to-high-risk primary membranous nephropathy. The MENTOR trial (NEJM 2019) showed sustained remission at 24 months in 60% on rituximab versus 20% on cyclosporine, with comparable safety. It is given as two 1 g IV infusions 2 weeks apart, sometimes repeated based on anti-PLA2R titer.

faqs.question: How long does treatment take for membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Supportive therapy with ACE inhibitor or ARB is continued indefinitely. Immunosuppression with rituximab or cyclophosphamide is given over weeks but takes 6-24 months to produce maximum remission. Sustained remission can last years; rituximab may be repeated based on rising anti-PLA2R titer or relapsing proteinuria.

faqs.question: What is the role of anti-PLA2R antibody?
faqs.answer: Anti-PLA2R antibody confirms primary MN with about 99% specificity and is positive in 70-80% of patients. Titers correlate with disease activity and predict response to treatment. Serial monitoring guides duration of therapy: falling titers precede proteinuria reduction by months and antibody-negative remissions are more stable.

faqs.question: Can membranous nephropathy lead to kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes, in 30-50% of untreated patients with sustained nephrotic-range proteinuria over 10 years. Risk-stratified treatment substantially reduces this rate. Predictors of progression include male sex, older age, proteinuria over 8 g/day, declining eGFR, high anti-PLA2R titer, and significant chronic injury on biopsy.

faqs.question: Why is screening for cancer important in older adults with MN?
faqs.answer: Up to 25% of MN cases in patients over 65 are paraneoplastic, most commonly with lung, colon, breast, or prostate cancers. NELL1-positive MN has particularly strong cancer association. Age-appropriate screening (chest CT, colonoscopy, mammography, PSA) is recommended at diagnosis, and cancer treatment often resolves the kidney disease.

faqs.question: Can hepatitis B cause membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic hepatitis B is the most common cause of secondary MN globally, particularly in endemic regions. HBV antigens form subepithelial immune complexes that damage the basement membrane. Treatment with entecavir or tenofovir alafenamide induces MN remission in many patients without additional immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What is the risk of blood clots in MN?
faqs.answer: Nephrotic syndrome creates a prothrombotic state by urinary loss of antithrombin and increased hepatic synthesis of fibrinogen. Renal vein thrombosis, deep-vein thrombosis, and pulmonary embolism affect up to 25% of patients with serum albumin under 25 g/L. Prophylactic anticoagulation is considered in high-risk patients using validated risk models.

faqs.question: Is membranous nephropathy hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases are not directly inherited, but strong HLA-DQA1 and PLA2R1 risk alleles increase susceptibility — the highest odds ratios in any common autoimmune disease. Family clustering is uncommon. Children with MN should be evaluated for inherited podocyte disorders and viral causes.

faqs.question: Can MN come back after a kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence in the allograft occurs in approximately 40% of patients with primary MN, often within the first 2 years post-transplant. Pre-transplant anti-PLA2R-positive recipients are at highest risk. Rituximab effectively induces remission in most recurrent cases.

faqs.question: Do I need to avoid certain medications with MN?
faqs.answer: Yes. NSAIDs and naproxen worsen kidney function and edema. Drug-induced MN can be caused by gold, penicillamine, captopril, mercury-containing creams, and certain biologic agents. Always review new prescriptions and supplements with your nephrologist, especially during immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What diet helps with membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Restrict salt to 2-3 g/day to control edema and blood pressure. Moderate protein intake (0.8-1.0 g/kg/day) is usually appropriate. Mediterranean-style eating with vegetables, fish, olive oil, and limited red meat supports cardiovascular health. Avoid grapefruit juice if on cyclosporine or tacrolimus. A renal dietitian helps individualize the plan.

faqs.question: Can membranous nephropathy occur in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes, though uncommon. Pregnancy can also cause MN-like findings in pre-eclampsia, which usually resolve after delivery. Confirmed MN in pregnancy is managed by nephrology and high-risk obstetrics with calcineurin inhibitors and careful blood pressure control; ACE inhibitors and rituximab are generally avoided.

faqs.question: How often is monitoring needed during remission?
faqs.answer: After remission, urine protein, serum creatinine, and serum albumin are checked every 3 months in the first year and every 6 months thereafter. Anti-PLA2R titer is repeated every 3-6 months for the first 2 years; rising titers prompt earlier re-evaluation. Long-term cardiovascular risk monitoring is essential.

faqs.question: Are calcineurin inhibitors still used for MN?
faqs.answer: Yes, as second-line therapy or in patients who cannot receive rituximab or cyclophosphamide. Cyclosporine and tacrolimus induce remission in 60-70% but have a high relapse rate after withdrawal and risk of nephrotoxicity. They are also used in pregnancy and post-transplant recurrence.

faqs.question: What is the Ponticelli regimen?
faqs.answer: The Ponticelli regimen alternates monthly courses of methylprednisolone pulses with oral steroid (months 1, 3, 5) and oral cyclophosphamide 2-2.5 mg/kg/day (months 2, 4, 6). It is highly effective (80-85% remission at 24 months in the original trial) and remains first-line in high-risk MN, especially with declining eGFR or unavailability of rituximab.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with membranous nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Yes, with adjustments. Moderate aerobic activity is encouraged once edema is controlled. Patients on anticoagulation should avoid contact sports. Resistance training helps maintain lean mass during steroid therapy. Restart progressively after rituximab infusions and report new symptoms or infections promptly.

metaDescription: Membranous nephropathy: PLA2R-driven nephrotic syndrome. Diagnosis, anti-PLA2R, rituximab and Ponticelli regimens, prognosis and supportive care explained.
